summary: The author achieved 3rd place by blending multiple versions of an LSTM neural network and an XGBoost model. Key to the approach was extensive feature engineering, deriving approximately 60 features like distances, angles, and speeds, and combining data across all labs by selecting five consistently present body parts. The pipeline used small batch training with a cosine decay schedule, followed by threshold tuning on out-of-fold predictions to convert blended probabilities into final binary actions.
extensive_summary:
The solution centers on a hybrid approach combining a neural network and gradient boosting. The neural network is an LSTM with several dense layers, outputting a softmax distribution over allowed actions with masking for invalid ones. A categorical embedding layer processes the "Laboratory code" and concatenates it to the inputs. To ensure the model generalizes across different experimental setups, the author combined input data from all labs and filtered for five body parts that appear consistently across the dataset.

Feature engineering was the primary focus, yielding approximately 60 hand-crafted features including distances, angles, speeds, and metadata. The XGBoost component was adapted from a strong public baseline but heavily modified: low-importance features were pruned, new features were added, and early stopping was implemented using the F1 metric. Hyperparameters were also optimized.

Training utilized small batches (16 or 32) and a CosineDecayRestarts learning rate schedule. The final submission was generated by blending multiple NN and XGB variants. Post-processing involved converting blended probabilities to binary predictions using thresholds optimized via grid search on out-of-fold predictions. These thresholds were specific to each lab and action. For frames with multiple predicted actions, the model retained the action with the highest probability-to-threshold ratio. The author also notes several data quirks (mislabeled actions, fixed action lengths) that could potentially be exploited for higher scores but were not leveraged in this submission.
competition_flow: Raw multi-lab data is filtered to five common body parts and combined, then enriched with ~60 hand-crafted features and categorical embeddings before being fed into blended LSTM and XGBoost models trained with small batches and cosine decay, followed by threshold-optimized probability blending for final binary predictions.
data_reading: Combined input data for all labs, selecting 5 body parts present in most of the data.
data_processing: Applied categorical embedding to 'Laboratory code', masked out invalid actions in softmax output, used small batches (16 or 32) and CosineDecayRestarts schedule during training.
features_engineering: Approximately 60 hand-crafted features including distances, angles, speeds, and metadata; categorical embedding for 'Laboratory code'.
cv_strategy: Out-of-fold predictions used for threshold optimization via grid search.
ensembling: Blended predicted probabilities from multiple NN and XGB versions, converted to binary predictions using lab- and action-specific thresholds optimized on OOF, and resolved frame conflicts by keeping the action with the highest probability-to-threshold ratio.
insights: Combining data across all labs and focusing on five consistently present body parts allows the model to learn robust patterns across different experimental setups.
Extensive feature engineering was more impactful than model complexity.
critical_findings: Some lab videos contain mislabeled actions that organizers did not correct.
Certain labs exhibit actions with fixed lengths (e.g., multiples of 15 frames for BoisterousParrot, exactly 9 frames for CautiousGiraffe with alternating mouse attacks).
what_did_not_work: Directly exploiting the noted data properties (mislabeled actions, fixed action lengths) did not yield significant benefits in this submission.

other_solution_refs.description: Discussion about mislabeled actions in AdaptableSnail lab
